title: What to Log in a RAG Pipeline
concept: Query, retrieved chunks with scores, final context, generated answer, and latency at every stage.

exercises.prompt: When building advanced LLM applications, how can developers inspect why a system is behaving poorly or running slowly?

options.text: By logging and measuring every step of the pipeline.
By asking users to rate every single chat message.
By making the LLM write its own code updates.

exercises.explain: This is the first lesson of the unit on Monitoring & Observability, introducing why we need to track internal pipeline steps to understand system behavior.

cards.title: Diagnosing LLMs
cards.body: When your AI app gives a wrong or slow answer, you can't just guess what went wrong. You need a clear paper trail of what the AI read, what it searched for, and how long each step took.

cards.title: RAG Logging
cards.body: Logging in a RAG pipeline means saving the inputs, intermediate search results (chunks and similarity scores), final outputs, and time taken (latency) for every single step of a user request.

cards.title: The Kitchen Inspection
cards.body: Think of a RAG pipeline like a restaurant kitchen. If a customer gets the wrong dish, you don't just blame the chef; you check the order ticket (query), the ingredients retrieved from the pantry (chunks and scores), and how long each step took (latency) to find where things went wrong.

cards.title: A Complete Log Entry
cards.body: When a user asks 'How to reset a password?', we log the query, the 3 retrieved document chunks with their 0.85 similarity scores, the assembled prompt, the LLM's final answer, and that retrieval took 50ms while generation took 800ms.

cards.title: The Final Answer Trap
cards.body: Many think logging just the final chatbot answer is enough. But without logging the retrieved chunks and latency at each step, you cannot tell if a bad answer was caused by poor search results or a confused LLM.

cards.title: Blind vs. Complete Logging
cards.body: Compare what you see when logging only the final answer versus logging the entire RAG pipeline process.

left.text: You only see 'Sorry, I don't know.' You have no idea if the database search failed or if the LLM ignored the data.

right.text: You see that search returned empty chunks (score 0.1) in 12ms. You instantly know the database retrieval is the problem!

exercises.prompt: Which of the following is essential to log from the retrieval stage of a RAG pipeline?

options.text: The retrieved chunks and their similarity scores.
The name of the database software.
The total number of users online.

exercises.explain: Retrieval similarity scores tell you how relevant the database search thinks the retrieved chunks are to the user query. Without this, you cannot judge retrieval quality.

exercises.prompt: True or False: To save space, you should discard the raw user query and only log the generated response.
exercises.explain: Logging the raw user query is essential because it allows developers to see exactly what the user asked and reproduce any errors.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about tracking pipeline performance.
exercises.sentence: To find out why a chatbot is slow, you must measure the latency of the retrieval and generation steps.
exercises.explain: Logging the latency (time taken) at every stage is crucial to find out if the bottleneck is in the database search or the LLM generation.

exercises.prompt: Which of these looks like a structured log generated by an AI observability system tracking a RAG pipeline?

options.text: We checked the database and it is running normally. The response time seems fine for most active users today.
User query was submitted successfully. The system generated a response in less than a second using standard search parameters.

exercises.explain: Option B is a clean, structured log capturing the query, retrieved context with relevance scores, generated answer, and latency metrics. The others are just general descriptions or irrelevant metrics.

exercises.prompt: You are building a dashboard to debug a slow and inaccurate RAG chatbot. Which set of logged data gives you the complete picture to solve both problems?

options.text: Only the final generated answer and the total time taken.
Only the user query and the retrieved database documents.
Every stage's latency, the query, the retrieved chunks with scores, and the generated response.
Only the similarity scores of the retrieved database chunks.

exercises.explain: To troubleshoot why a system is slow or incorrect, you need both the performance metrics (latency at each step) and the actual data (query, chunks, and final response) to pinpoint the exact failure location.
